result:
There is a moment, brief as a flashlight blink, when the world feels evaporated down to its essence. I notice it most often on the third Wednesday of September, because that is the day my city’s river glints at such an angle that the sun throws every boat mast into high relief and the water appears liquid nickel. It seems absurd to assign such specificity to wonder, as though the cosmos kept a pocket calendar: *2:17 p.m., unveil beauty to the girl on the pedestrian bridge.* Yet there it is, and for a second my mind empties of overdue tax forms and half-finished text messages, of all the brittle accretions that pass for modern life. The hush is so sudden I almost hear my ribs creak.

Then the traffic light changes and a cyclist curses at a turning sedan and the moment collapses like an origami swan dunked in water, leaving only soggy paper. I resume my walk to the pharmacy where fluorescent lights will erase every shadow and the piped-in pop cover of “Mad World” will remind me I am nowhere near a desert island. Still, I keep rewinding that sliver of nickel river in my head, the way a child tongues a loose tooth, half worried, half thrilled. I suspect this private looping is what we mean when we claim to “have a life”: the compilation of instants we replay when the external world bores or bruises us. Everything else is just administration.

I write these words on an old laptop missing the letter X. I have to copy-and-paste each X like a ransom note, which makes threatening text messages curiously difficult. People assume I’m being flirtatious when I send “I’m gonna ki$$ you,” not realizing I’m actually promising homicide. This small technological amputation fascinates me more than it frustrates. Once you notice an absence, it multiplies: the missing oarlock in a rental boat, the vanished church bell after the lightning strike, the ex-lover who stole your OutKast T-shirt. Absence is a negative space that grows teeth. It teaches you that the world was never whole; it was only pretending, and you believed the performance because the lights were dim.

When I was ten, my father told me that cassette tapes worked by arranging iron filings inside a plastic ribbon. One magnetic shiver and Beethoven could inhabit kitchen curtains, or a thrash-metal band could scream between the bucket seats of a Chevette. I pictured tiny iron soldiers standing at attention, ready to rearrange themselves into whichever song the universe requested. That may have been my first brush with what the poet called “the thingness of things,” the stubborn miracle that matter remembers. Even now, decades after CDs flattened music into rainbows, I half expect my MP3s to harbor metaphysical grit, a residue of whatever basement show or cathedral became data. When the streaming service skips, I imagine some wronged iron filing rebelling against its new life as code.

Language itself behaves like those soldiers. Tomorrow the word “random” might feel as dated as “groovy,” and yet today we keep deploying it to describe everything from pineapple on pizza to asteroid trajectories. I remember when “literally” began its slide into hyperbole; it felt like watching a dignified aunt get drunk at a wedding and dance barefoot on a table. The descriptivists insist this is natural, that English is a river, not a museum. Still, I mourn the miniature extinction each time a word’s definition dilutes. “Awesome” once implied terror of an approaching god; now it signals approval of a taco. The iron filings shuffle, and we keep dancing because the song is still recognizable even when the key has changed.

The paradox is that I make my living trafficking in words, spinning them like cotton candy for clients who need “content.” I am a hired tongue, paid to say “innovative solutions” without rolling my eyes. Freelance writing is the only occupation where you can be both underfed and obese: underfed from inconsistent paychecks, obese from sitting sixteen hours in sweatpants, growing mossy with caffeine. The gig economy promises freedom, but mostly it delivers a calendar striped with color-coded deadlines resembling a bruise. Still, I guard certain sentences the way a bouncer shields velvet rope. I keep a private file labeled “DO NOT SELL,” full of phrases that taste like the river at 2:17 p.m.—lines about “the lisp of snow on antennae” or “how headlights baptize a garage door.” No one will ever commission those lines for a branding package, and that is their salvation.

Sometimes I fantasize about deleting every social profile and moving to a town where the post office closes for lunch. I would learn which wood burns sweetest and how to identify chanterelles without poisoning myself. I would own one plate, one bowl, one mug the color of robins’ eggs, and I would write letters by lamplight to the three people who still remember my middle name. Within six months I would probably be screaming into a pillow from boredom, but the fantasy persists, refreshed each time a targeted ad follows me across the internet like an orphaned duckling. Capitalism has grown so intimate it wishes me happy birthday before my mother does; it recommends coffins based on browser history. I click “purchase” because resistance is exhausting and also because the coffin has free shipping.

I read that the universe will end not with fire but with ice—slow, diluted ice, everything drifting so far apart that particles forget to collide. The stars will wink out like hostel lights after curfew, and all the music ever recorded will submerge into absolute zero silence. Sometimes, on insomniac nights, I picture those final moments: the last iron filing losing grip, the last magnetized echo of a human voice asking, “Are you still there?” In that hush, the distinction between literal and figurative evaporates; everything becomes the nickel river. Perhaps the void is not an emptiness but a transparency, a return to the instant before language rented space in our mouths. The thought should horrify me, yet it arrive also as relief, the way dropping a heavy bag at journey’s end floods the shoulders with ache and lightness simultaneously.

Which brings me back to the bridge, the cyclist, the cursed sedan. I still have seven hundred iron filings to arrange before deadline, and the pharmacy is about to close. My laptop fan whirs like a distant lawn mower. I copy and paste one more X, paste it into the word “exist,” and for a heartbeat the soldiers stand at attention, ready to sing.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
